questions.question: Apache Spark processes data by:
options: Distributing data and computation across a cluster in parallel
questions.explanation: Spark partitions data across nodes. Each node processes its partition simultaneously.

questions.question: Spark's lazy evaluation means:
options: Transformations are not executed until an action (collect, show, count) is called
questions.explanation: Spark builds an execution plan then optimises and executes only when an action triggers it.

questions.question: Spark DataFrames are preferred over RDDs because:
options: DataFrames have Catalyst optimiser support, SQL syntax, and strongly typed schemas
questions.explanation: Catalyst optimiser automatically rewrites queries for better performance.

questions.question: PySpark groupBy().agg() is equivalent to:
options: SQL GROUP BY with aggregate functions -- runs distributed across the cluster
questions.explanation: Same semantics as SQL/pandas groupby but runs on terabytes across many nodes.

questions.question: Spark partitions data for:
options: Parallel processing -- each partition is processed by one executor core
questions.explanation: repartition(n) controls parallelism. More partitions = more parallelism.

questions.question: Spark broadcast variables are used for:
options: Distributing small datasets (like lookup tables) efficiently to all worker nodes
questions.explanation: broadcast(small_df) avoids shipping the same small data to each node per task.

questions.question: Spark Streaming vs batch Spark:
options: Batch: processes fixed datasets. Streaming: processes continuous data streams in micro-batches
questions.explanation: Structured Streaming provides the same DataFrame API for streaming as for batch.

questions.question: The driver program in Spark:
options: Coordinates the cluster, creates SparkContext, and sends tasks to executors
questions.explanation: One driver + many executors. Driver is the brain; executors do the work.

questions.question: When should you use Spark over Pandas?
options: When data exceeds single-machine RAM (~10-50GB+) or you need distributed processing
questions.explanation: Pandas is simpler and faster for small data. Spark overhead is only worth it for large datasets.

questions.question: A Spark DataFrame's schema is:
options: Strictly typed with named columns -- enables optimiser to plan efficient execution
questions.explanation: Strong typing enables Catalyst optimiser and avoids runtime type errors.
